meta.title: AI: Strategy, Ethics & the Age of Generative Intelligence

slides.headline: AI: Strategy, Ethics & the Age of Generative Intelligence
Generative AI: Fastest Consumer Adoption in History
slides.subheadline: ChatGPT reached 50% US market penetration faster than smartphones, TV, or the internet.

slides.label: Market Context
slides.headline: Will AI Be the Next Technology Revolution?
slides.caption: Every decade, paradigm shifts lead to massive redistribution of profits and new market leaders. Source: Prof. Andy Wu, Harvard Business School

slides.label: Hardware Economics
slides.headline: Huang's Law: GPU Performance Doubles — Price Collapses
slides.caption: Transistor counts grow exponentially while cost per FLOP falls — the hardware foundation making Gen AI possible. Source: Prof. Andy Wu, Harvard Business School

slides.label: Data Explosion
slides.headline: Global Data Generation: 2 ZB to 181 ZB in 15 Years
slides.caption: The fuel for AI. Volume of accessible data has grown 90× since 2010 and continues to accelerate. Source: Prof. Andy Wu, Harvard Business School

slides.label: Access Revolution
slides.headline: AI Changes Who Can Access Specialised Software
slides.caption: Previously, each worker needed to learn a specific tool (SAP, Photoshop, Excel). AI abstraction means one person can direct many — collapsing the traditional access hierarchy. Source: Prof. Andy Wu, Harvard Business School

slides.headline: Two Ways AI Augments Computing
slides.subheadline: The transformative shift is on the input side — who can access software — not just what it produces.
bullets: Inputs — Abstraction: natural language replaces formal commands; anyone can now use specialised software
Outputs — Computation: AI expands what software produces — text, code, images, autonomous decisions
Interface evolution — Mathematics → Formal Text → Graphics → Natural Language: the access barrier disappears
Net effect — More people using more software for more specialised purposes — but not the same people

slides.headline: The AI Adoption Matrix
slides.subheadline: Where should humans stay in the loop?

quadrants.label: Creative Catalyst
quadrants.desc: Tacit knowledge + low error cost → AI creates options, human selects

quadrants.label: Human First
quadrants.desc: Tacit knowledge + high error cost → Human leads and does the heavy lifting

quadrants.label: No Regrets
quadrants.desc: Explicit data + low error cost → AI acts autonomously, no human in loop

quadrants.label: Quality Control
quadrants.desc: Explicit data + high error cost → AI produces work, human verifies

slides.headline: AI's Primary Strategic Impact: Cost Structure
slides.subheadline: Most debate focuses on quality. The more decisive lever is cost.
bullets: Worth to Customer — AI can raise quality: but this is uncertain, debated, and often incremental
Cost to Supplier — AI cuts the cost base: this is measurable, immediate, and structurally decisive
The Ryanair principle — cheaper beats better: lower cost at comparable value wins more customers
Adopt even when imperfect — low error cost + abundant data justifies AI agents even with quality gaps

slides.label: Market Segmentation
slides.headline: Even a Slightly Different Product Captures a Whole New Market
slides.caption: Most consumers can't distinguish between nearly identical products — but the minority who can be reached at low marginal cost represents a structurally profitable segment. This is the AI competitive dynamic. Source: Prof. Andy Wu, Harvard Business School

slides.headline: Industry Disruption: The Blockbuster Warning
slides.subheadline: Wide AI availability lowers barriers to entry — threatening incumbents who hesitate.
bullets: Blockbuster knew — In 2000 they explored online DVD and Video-on-Demand, but didn't fully commit
Netflix was tiny — In 2003 Blockbuster launched a Netflix clone; Netflix was still 1.5% of their size
Bankruptcy 2010 — $1 billion in debt. Recognition without action is not a strategy
AI replicates this dynamic — lower barriers let leaner, bolder entrants undercut incumbents on cost
Self-disruption is survival — calculated risk today beats cautious optimisation of a shrinking position

slides.headline: Strategic Transformation: Two Imperatives
slides.subheadline: Winners redesign the whole organisation — not just bolt on new tools.
bullets: Reevaluate all assets as data: Not just digital systems — physical processes, relationships, expertise all become training signal
Redesign from first principles: Don't layer AI onto existing workstreams. Rethink who does what and why
Act now, not later: Waiting for AI to mature cedes first-mover advantage to competitors who move first
Choose your mode deliberately: Customise Model · Integrate APIs · Purchase Applications — each has different lock-in
Create more value than rivals: Long-term profitability demands out-innovating competitors with AI — or losing ground

slides.headline: Ethics & AI
slides.subheadline: The principles that must guide development and use

slides.headline: What Is AI Ethics?
slides.subheadline: Moral principles guiding how AI is built and deployed — not a compliance checkbox.
bullets: Benefit society: AI systems must serve broader human interests, not only shareholders or developers
Five core pillars: Fairness, Transparency, Privacy, Safety and Accountability guide every design decision
Bias at scale: AI learns from data — biased data encodes and amplifies human injustice across millions of decisions
High-stakes delegation: Medical, legal, and financial decisions are already being made by or with AI models
Responsible direction: Ethics is the mechanism that steers AI development toward socially beneficial outcomes

slides.headline: Seven Ethical Principles for AI Development
slides.subheadline: Governance without societal grounding is incomplete — and vice versa.

left.title: Governance Pillars
bullets: Human Agency & Oversight — humans must own final decisions
Technical Robustness & Safety — errors must not lead to harm
Privacy & Data Governance — strict rules on personal data use
Transparency — explain decisions, steps, and data sources

right.title: Societal Pillars
bullets: Diversity, Non-Discrimination & Fairness — AI must work equally for everyone
Societal & Environmental Wellbeing — AI must deliver a positive net impact
Accountability — clear ownership when systems cause harm or fail

slides.label: Real-World Failure
slides.headline: The ChatGPT Lawyer: AI Hallucination in a Court of Law
slides.caption: A lawyer relied on AI to craft legal motions full of made-up case law, saying he 'did not comprehend' the chatbot could lead him astray. Faced court sanctions. Source: The New York Times, June 2023

slides.headline: Real-World Ethical Failures
slides.subheadline: These are not hypothetical — they are happening now.
bullets: Judicial AI — A judge used ChatGPT to advise on a child's medical rights and was ruled unethical by an oversight body
Hallucination in court — A lawyer submitted AI-generated fake case citations; they faced sanctions and public censure
Social Credit — China's 1,000-point AI-monitored system rewards and punishes 14 million+ citizens based on behaviour
Environmental cost — Data centres consumed 22% of Ireland's energy in 2024, growing with every additional AI prompt
Water usage — ChatGPT uses approximately 0.5 litres of water per 5–50 prompts for server cooling alone

slides.label: Labour Market Shift
slides.headline: Bargaining Power: In-House Legal Grew 200%+ as Firms Cut External Spend
slides.caption: As AI tools enabled companies to bring legal work in-house, law firm growth stagnated. This pattern — technology shifting bargaining power from specialist suppliers to buyers — is now repeating across every knowledge profession. Source: Prof. Andy Wu, Harvard Business School

slides.headline: AI and the Future of Work
slides.subheadline: Automation now reaches white-collar professions — not just physical labour.

stats.label: Growth in US in-house legal roles (1997–2019) as firms cut external law firm spend
Marginal cost of an additional AI-generated legal document, code review, or brief
YC Gen AI startups targeting engineering, product and design — all cognitive roles
Key questions: Where is our data? What must our organisation look like?

slides.label: Provocation
slides.headline: The Last Job on Earth
slides.subheadline: Imagining a fully automated world — 2 min 52 sec
slides.caption: A short film exploring what a world without human labour might look like, and what it means for how we think about AI strategy today.

slides.subheadline: Strategy and ethics are not in tension — both demand anticipation and the courage to act.
bullets: Anticipate the curve: Generative AI will keep improving — leaders must plan for the trajectory, not the current state
Access is the revolution: AI changes who can use specialised computing — democratising and disrupting simultaneously
Cost beats quality: Even weak AI is worth adopting when structural cost savings outweigh quality trade-offs
Disrupt yourself first: Wide AI adoption intensifies competition — incumbents who wait become the Blockbuster
Ethics is not optional: Bias, privacy, and accountability require proactive governance — not reactive compliance
Two questions: Where is our data? What must our organisation look like to compete in five years?

slides.headline: The Age of AI Demands Both Strategy and Ethics
slides.subheadline: The organisations and societies that thrive will be those that act with both intelligence and integrity.
